agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "New 'Express Delivery' product offering faster delivery with a time-window commitment and premium fee. Synthesized from raw product discovery workshop notes involving PM, Operations, Finance, Customer Success, and Legal stakeholders. Contains fundamental unresolved contradictions between stakeholders that must be resolved before implementation.",
    "bounded_context": "Express Delivery Fulfillment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Express Delivery", "definition": "A premium delivery option with a 2-hour delivery window and additional fee, subject to eligibility constraints (distance, driver rating)." },
      { "term": "Express Fee", "definition": "The premium surcharge ($12.99) charged for express delivery, separate from the base delivery fee." },
      { "term": "Base Delivery Fee", "definition": "The standard delivery fee charged on all orders, not subject to express refund." },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The 2-hour time period within which the express delivery is expected to be completed. DISPUTED: PM calls it a 'guarantee'; Legal calls it 'best effort SLA'." },
      { "term": "Pickup SLA", "definition": "The 15-minute maximum time from order placement to physical pickup by a driver, required for the delivery window to remain achievable." },
      { "term": "Express-Eligible Driver", "definition": "A driver with a rating of at least 4.0 stars who is qualified to fulfill express orders." },
      { "term": "Service Radius", "definition": "The 10km maximum distance between the customer's delivery address and a warehouse, required for express eligibility." },
      { "term": "Goodwill Refund", "definition": "Legal's term for the express fee refund on missed delivery windows — explicitly not a contractual obligation." },
      { "term": "Driver Replacement Window", "definition": "The 10-minute period within which a replacement driver must be assigned after a mid-delivery cancellation before an automatic refund is triggered." },
      { "term": "Wrong Address", "definition": "A customer-provided address that is incorrect or undeliverable. UNDEFINED: no criteria exist for who determines this or what constitutes 'wrong'." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Express delivery option is only available to customers whose delivery address is within 10km of a warehouse.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 10km radius is a business policy threshold that could be adjusted as the delivery network scales. A VP could change this to 8km or 15km based on operational data.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer address more than 10km from any warehouse, the express delivery option must not be selectable."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The express fee is $12.99, charged in addition to the base delivery fee.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing is a business policy decision, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an express order, the total delivery charge equals the base delivery fee plus $12.99."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An express order must be picked up by a driver within 15 minutes of order placement.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 15-minute threshold is an operational policy derived from the 2-hour window math. It could be tuned based on logistics data.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an express order not picked up within 15 minutes, the system must escalate (exact escalation behavior TBD — see escalation E-3)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Only drivers with a rating of 4.0 stars or above may be assigned to express deliveries.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 4-star threshold is a quality policy that could be adjusted based on driver pool availability.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with a rating below 4.0 stars, the system must not assign them to an express order."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "When an express delivery misses the delivery window, the express fee is refunded but the base delivery fee is not.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Refund scope is a business policy. Whether to also refund the base fee is a commercial decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an express delivery completed after the 2-hour window, a refund of exactly $12.99 (the express fee) is initiated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Express fee refunds are not issued if the delivery window was missed due to a wrong address provided by the customer.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a refund policy exception. The definition of 'wrong address' and adjudication process are undefined (see escalation E-2).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a missed express delivery where the cause is attributed to 'wrong address', no express fee refund is initiated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Express fee refunds must be processed within 3 business days of the missed delivery window.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Processing timeline is a finance policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an approved express fee refund, the refund transaction must be completed within 3 business days."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "If a driver cancels mid-delivery on an express order, a replacement driver must be assigned within 10 minutes. If no replacement is assigned within 10 minutes, the express fee is automatically refunded.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 10-minute replacement window and automatic refund trigger are operational policies.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver cancellation on an active express delivery where 10 minutes elapse without a replacement assignment, an express fee refund is automatically initiated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Express customers must be able to track their assigned driver's location in real time.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time tracking is a product feature requirement, not a system invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active express delivery, the customer can view the driver's current location updated at least every 30 seconds (frequency TBD)."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Customer is exactly 10km from a warehouse", "expected_behavior": "Boundary condition: must define whether 10km is inclusive or exclusive. Recommend inclusive (<=10km) for customer-friendly behavior." },
    { "scenario": "No express-eligible drivers (4+ stars) are available at order time", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Should the express option be hidden, or shown with a 'currently unavailable' state? This affects customer expectations." },
    { "scenario": "Driver picks up at 14 minutes but delivery takes longer than 2 hours", "expected_behavior": "The pickup SLA was met, but the delivery window was still missed. Refund should still apply per BR-5 since the window is customer-facing." },
    { "scenario": "Customer provides an address that is technically valid but inaccessible (gated community, construction zone)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Does this count as a 'wrong address'? Finance's exception in BR-6 has no clear adjudication criteria." },
    { "scenario": "Driver cancels at minute 115 of a 120-minute window", "expected_behavior": "The 10-minute replacement window (BR-8) extends past the 2-hour delivery window. Both BR-5 (missed window) and BR-8 (replacement timeout) may trigger simultaneously. Refund should not be doubled." },
    { "scenario": "Customer moves to a new address after placing an express order", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. If the new address is >10km from the warehouse, does eligibility retroactively invalidate? Can the delivery window restart?" },
    { "scenario": "Order placed at 11:55 PM — 2-hour window spans midnight", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Are there operating hours for express delivery? The '3 business days' refund language implies business-hours awareness exists somewhere." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple warehouses within 10km — which warehouse is used for distance calculation?", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Presumably the nearest warehouse, but this affects pickup logistics and driver assignment." },
    { "scenario": "Express order with pickup completed but driver's app loses GPS signal", "expected_behavior": "Real-time tracking (BR-9) becomes impossible. Must define fallback UX — stale location with timestamp? Status message?" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Express fee of $12.99 creates a new revenue stream but carries refund liability. Automatic refunds on driver cancellation (BR-8) and missed windows (BR-5) create unpredictable cost exposure. The 'wrong address' exception (BR-6) is the only defined refund gate, and without clear adjudication criteria it will either be under-applied (costing money) or over-applied (damaging trust). The contradiction between 'guarantee' and 'best effort' language directly affects refund volume — a guarantee sets customer expectations that drive refund claims.",
    "operational": "The 15-minute pickup SLA (BR-3) and 10-minute replacement window (BR-8) create tight operational constraints requiring high driver density and real-time dispatch optimization. The 4-star driver requirement (BR-4) constrains the eligible driver pool. Combined, these rules may make express delivery operationally infeasible in low-density areas even within the 10km radius.",
    "user": "Customers will perceive 'guaranteed 2 hours' as a firm commitment regardless of legal fine print. Prominent display of the guarantee (per Customer Success) paired with 'best effort' disclaimers (per Legal) will create cognitive dissonance and erode trust when windows are missed. Real-time tracking raises the bar for transparency — customers watching a stalled driver icon will contact support before the window even expires.",
    "risk": "HIGH. The guarantee vs. best-effort contradiction is a reputational and potential regulatory risk. Advertising a '2-hour guarantee' while legally disclaiming it could attract consumer protection scrutiny in multiple jurisdictions. The undefined 'wrong address' adjudication process is a customer dispute risk. Automatic refund triggers without fraud controls create abuse potential.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "New premium option with time commitment. Confusing guarantee language may set incorrect expectations. Real-time tracking improves transparency." },
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "4-star rating requirement gates access to express orders (potentially higher-value). 15-minute pickup SLA creates time pressure. Mid-delivery cancellation triggers replacement flow." },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Must build real-time driver assignment with tight SLAs (15min pickup, 10min replacement). Need driver availability and rating filtering in assignment logic." },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "New revenue stream with refund liability. Must track express fees separately for refund processing. 3-business-day processing SLA requires refund queue management." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will field complaints about missed windows, 'wrong address' disputes, and guarantee vs. best-effort confusion. Need clear escalation paths and adjudication authority." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance", "impact": "Must review all customer-facing language to ensure 'guarantee' is not used if the SLA is best-effort. Refund policy must be clearly communicated pre-purchase." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 35,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is very low because there is a fundamental unresolved contradiction at the core of the product: PM and Customer Success treat the 2-hour window as a guarantee with prominent display and automatic refunds, while Legal explicitly states it cannot be a guarantee and refunds are goodwill gestures. This contradiction affects the product's entire identity, customer communication, refund logic, and legal exposure. Until this is resolved at the executive level, acceptance criteria cannot be reliably defined. Additionally, multiple gaps exist around adjudication processes, operating hours, and escalation behaviors.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Express fee amount ($12.99) is consistently stated",
      "10km service radius is clear and uncontested",
      "4-star driver rating threshold is clear",
      "Base delivery fee is not refunded — all parties agree",
      "Real-time tracking is a requirement"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the 2-hour window is a guarantee or best-effort SLA",
      "Whether refunds are automatic obligations or discretionary goodwill",
      "What constitutes a 'wrong address' and who adjudicates",
      "What happens when pickup SLA is breached (escalation undefined)",
      "Operating hours for express delivery",
      "How the 10-minute replacement and 2-hour window interact at boundary conditions",
      "Whether express availability depends on real-time driver pool or just distance"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The 10km distance is calculated as straight-line (Euclidean) from warehouse to delivery address, not driving distance — this is unspecified",
      "The 2-hour window starts at order placement, not at pickup — this is unspecified but implied by the 15-minute pickup being carved from the total",
      "Driver rating is a rolling average, not a per-delivery score — rating calculation method is unspecified",
      "Only one refund is issued per order regardless of how many refund triggers fire (missed window + replacement timeout should not stack)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "FUNDAMENTAL CONFLICT: PM and Customer Success describe the 2-hour delivery window as a 'guarantee' with automatic refunds on breach. Legal explicitly states this cannot be a guarantee in writing — it is a 'best effort SLA' and the refund is a 'goodwill gesture, not legally enforceable.' These positions are irreconcilable without executive decision. This affects every customer-facing string, refund trigger design, and the product's market positioning.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal / Compliance", "Customer Support", "Finance", "Marketing"],
      "options": [
        "A: Treat as a guarantee — accept legal risk, auto-refund on all breaches, use 'guaranteed' in customer-facing language",
        "B: Treat as best-effort — use softer language ('expected within 2 hours'), make refunds discretionary via support request, reduce customer expectations",
        "C: Hybrid — use 'commitment' language (not 'guarantee'), auto-refund as a policy (not a contractual right), include terms of service disclaimer"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C (Hybrid). Use language like '2-Hour Express Commitment' rather than 'guarantee.' Auto-refund as standard policy but reserve the right to deny in abuse/fraud cases. Include clear terms of service. This satisfies customer expectations while preserving legal flexibility. However, this is a business/legal decision that must be made by product and legal leadership together."
    },
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "Customer Success states that if no replacement driver is assigned within 10 minutes of a mid-delivery cancellation, the refund is 'automatic.' Legal states refunds are 'goodwill gestures, not legally enforceable.' If automatic refund triggers are built into the system, they become de facto obligations regardless of legal framing. Engineering cannot build both 'automatic' and 'discretionary' — the system either triggers the refund or it doesn't.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal / Compliance", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Build automatic refund trigger as Customer Success requests — accept that it becomes a de facto policy",
        "B: Build a refund recommendation that requires manual approval — preserves discretion but adds operational burden",
        "C: Build automatic trigger with an admin override/hold capability for fraud or abuse cases"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. Automatic by default with admin override preserves the customer experience while giving Finance and Legal a safety valve."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Finance states no refund if 'customer gave a wrong address' but provides no definition of what constitutes a wrong address, no adjudication process, no evidence standard, and no timeline for dispute resolution. Is a typo in an apartment number a 'wrong address'? What about an address that exists but is gated/inaccessible? Who decides — the driver, support, an automated system?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define 'wrong address' as an address that does not exist or cannot be geocoded — narrow, automated, customer-friendly",
        "B: Define 'wrong address' as any address the driver cannot deliver to, including inaccessible locations — broad, driver-discretion, dispute-prone",
        "C: Define tiers — 'invalid address' (auto-deny refund) vs 'inaccessible address' (case-by-case review)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. Tiered approach balances automation with fairness. Invalid/non-existent addresses are auto-classified; accessibility issues go to support review. But this needs a formal policy document from Customer Success and Finance."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No stakeholder defined what happens when the 15-minute pickup SLA is breached. Does the order get cancelled? Downgraded to standard delivery with refund of express fee? Reassigned to another driver? Does the customer get notified? This is a critical operational flow with no defined behavior.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations / Dispatch", "Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Auto-reassign to next available express-eligible driver, restart 15-minute clock",
        "B: Notify customer of delay and offer cancellation of express upgrade with fee refund",
        "C: Auto-reassign once, then downgrade to standard delivery with express fee refund if second assignment also fails"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. One retry preserves the express experience; automatic downgrade with refund prevents indefinite waiting. But Operations must confirm this is feasible given driver pool constraints."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No operating hours defined for express delivery. The 3-business-day refund timeline implies business-hours awareness, but can a customer place an express order at 2 AM? Are there blackout periods (holidays, extreme weather)? This affects warehouse staffing, driver availability, and customer expectations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations / Dispatch", "Warehouse Staff"],
      "options": [
        "A: Express available 24/7 — requires round-the-clock staffing",
        "B: Express available during defined hours (e.g., 8 AM - 10 PM) — simpler operations",
        "C: Express availability is dynamic based on real-time driver and warehouse capacity"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Need input from Operations on staffing feasibility before recommending. Option C is most realistic but most complex to build."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "When does the 2-hour delivery window start? At order placement? At payment confirmation? At driver assignment? At pickup? This is critical — if the window starts at placement and pickup takes 15 minutes, the driver has only 105 minutes for transit and delivery. If it starts at pickup, the driver has the full 120 minutes. The Operations Lead's 15-minute comment implies placement, but this is not explicit.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations / Dispatch", "Drivers", "Customer Support"],
      "options": [
        "A: Window starts at order placement (customer's perspective — most intuitive)",
        "B: Window starts at pickup (driver's perspective — most operationally fair)",
        "C: Window starts at payment confirmation (system event — most precise)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. The customer's clock starts when they place the order. This is the only interpretation that matches customer expectations. Operations must plan around this constraint, not redefine the window to be more convenient internally."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Cannot define acceptance criteria when the core product promise (guarantee vs. best-effort) is contradicted between stakeholders.", "business_rule_broken": "PM's 'guaranteed within 2 hours' directly contradicts Legal's 'best effort SLA, not a contractual guarantee'. This must be resolved before any implementation begins." },
    { "violation": "Automatic refund behavior cannot be implemented when two stakeholders (Customer Success and Legal) have irreconcilable positions on whether refunds are automatic rights or discretionary goodwill.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5 and BR-8 refund triggers cannot be coded as both 'automatic' and 'discretionary'." }
  ]
}
